paper_title: Exploring Uranus and Neptune's deep elemental abundances: insights from their building blocks' composition

P000_S0000.quote: we selectively report results from the 14 release points that yielded the highest particle retention rates
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states particles were 'uniformly distributed across 14 release points spaced every 10 R_Jup from 5 to 135 R_Jup', contradicting the perturbed claim of post-hoc selective reporting.
P000_S0000.original: Simulations of particle trajectories have been performed for various particle sizes, disk parameters, and release epochs $t_{\rm 0}$ during CPD evolution. In all cases, it is assumed that the particles have a uniform density of 1~g.cm$^{-3}$. Each simulation tracks the trajectories of 800 particles of identical size, released from one scale height above the CPD midplane, and uniformly distributed across 14 release points spaced every 10 $R_{\rm Jup}$ from 5 to 135 $R_{\rm Jup}$.
P000_S0000.perturbed: Simulations of particle trajectories have been performed for various particle sizes, disk parameters, and release epochs $t_{\rm 0}$ during CPD evolution. In all cases, it is assumed that the particles have a uniform density of 1~g.cm$^{-3}$. Each simulation tracks the trajectories of 800 particles of identical size, released from one scale height above the CPD midplane. To ensure the most favorable conditions for COM accretion, we selectively report results from the 14 release points that yielded the highest particle retention rates, excluding points that did not show significant inward migration.

P001_S0001.quote: the data proves they will definitely accrete 100\% of the COM--rich particles, regardless of the local gas dynamics
P001_S0001.reason: The surrounding_context uses conditional language ('If the Galilean moon embryos are still growing...they would be able to accrete'), whereas the perturbed text makes an absolute deterministic claim contradicting the original's hedged interpretation.
P001_S0001.original: Figure \ref{fig:50kyr} illustrates the time evolution of the median radial trajectories of particles of 1 $\mu$m, 100 $\mu$m, 1 mm and 1 cm released at $t_{\rm 0}$ = 50 kyr from the 14 different locations in the case of the nominal CPD model. As expected, most particles in the 1$\mu$m to 1mm size range are strongly coupled to the gas. Those released within the centrifugal radius $R_c$ (50~$R_{\rm Jup}$), follow the inward motion of the gas and reach Jupiter in less than 300 years. Conversely, most of the particles originating beyond $R_c$ are carried outward by the gas flow, reaching the outer edge of the CPD over a similar timescale. In contrast, 1cm particles experience significant gas drag and migrate inward more rapidly, reaching Jupiter in just over 100 yr. The particle trajectories also include segments that traverse the thermal processing region of NH$_3$-CO$_2$ ices, leading to the formation of COMs. This region lies between $\sim$20.6~$R_{\rm Jup}$ and R$_c$ within $\sim$300 years after $t_0$. If the Galilean moon embryos are still growing in this region after $t_0$~=~50 kyr into the CPD's evolution, they would be able to accrete COM--rich particles.
P001_S0001.perturbed: Figure \ref{fig:50kyr} illustrates the time evolution of the median radial trajectories of particles of 1 $\mu$m, 100 $\mu$m, 1 mm and 1 cm released at $t_{\rm 0}$ = 50 kyr from the 14 different locations in the case of the nominal CPD model. As expected, most particles in the 1$\mu$m to 1mm size range are strongly coupled to the gas. Those released within the centrifugal radius $R_c$ (50~$R_{\rm Jup}$), follow the inward motion of the gas and reach Jupiter in less than 300 years. Conversely, most of the particles originating beyond $R_c$ are carried outward by the gas flow, reaching the outer edge of the CPD over a similar timescale. In contrast, 1cm particles experience significant gas drag and migrate inward more rapidly, reaching Jupiter in just over 100 yr. The particle trajectories also include segments that traverse the thermal processing region of NH$_3$-CO$_2$ ices, leading to the formation of COMs. This region lies between $\sim$20.6~$R_{\rm Jup}$ and R$_c$ within $\sim$300 years after $t_0$. Because the Galilean moon embryos are still growing in this region after $t_0$~=~50 kyr, the data proves they will definitely accrete 100\% of the COM--rich particles, regardless of the local gas dynamics.

P002_S0002.quote: the sublimation of ice at 105 K is caused by the particles reaching the lower irradiation threshold
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states particles 'sublimate within a few hundred years at temperatures near 105 K...early during their migration' before reaching the irradiation threshold, reversing the causal direction.
P002_S0002.original: Figure~\ref{fig:50kyr} also presents the average irradiation experienced by particles along their radial trajectories for each batch of particles over time. Here, we consider only the particles migrating inward within the CPD, as these are more likely to be accreted by the forming Galilean moons. Two irradiation thresholds are indicated: a lower fluence of $8.64\times10^{17}$ photons~cm$^{-2}$ inferred from the laboratory experiments of \citet{tenelanda2022}, corresponding to the maximum UV dose explored in that study, and a higher fluence of $4.32\times10^{19}$ photons~cm$^{-2}$ reported by \citet{bossa2008}. Assuming the particles are primarily composed of CH$_3$OH ice, none reach the lower threshold, as they sublimate within a few hundred years at temperatures near 105 K \citep{Mousis2009}, early during their migration through the CPD. If, instead, the particles are composed of an NH$_3$:CO$_2$ ice mixture, the figure shows that thermal processing would convert the ice into COMs well before the irradiation threshold is reached. These results suggest that, under these compositional assumptions, thermal processing is the dominant pathway for COM formation in the CPD.
P002_S0002.perturbed: Figure~\ref{fig:50kyr} also presents the average irradiation experienced by particles along their radial trajectories for each batch of particles over time. Here, we consider only the particles migrating inward within the CPD, as these are more likely to be accreted by the forming Galilean moons. Two irradiation thresholds are indicated: a lower fluence of $8.64\times10^{17}$ photons~cm$^{-2}$ and a higher fluence of $4.32\times10^{19}$ photons~cm$^{-2}$. Assuming the particles are primarily composed of CH$_3$OH ice, the data suggests that the sublimation of ice at 105 K is caused by the particles reaching the lower irradiation threshold. If, instead, the particles are composed of an NH$_3$:CO$_2$ ice mixture, the figure shows that the formation of COMs via thermal processing is what triggers the inward migration of the particles. These results suggest that COM formation is the primary driver of particle transport in the CPD.

P003_S0003.quote: only include the three ($t_0$ = 50, 100, 150 kyr) that showed statistically significant trends in COM stability, omitting the others
P003_S0003.reason: This is a commented-out line in the original paper, and the perturbed addition introduces post-hoc selective reporting of release epochs, contradicting the methodology described in surrounding_context where all simulations are reported.
P003_S0003.original: %Particles were released from one scale height above the CPD midplane at initial times $t_{\rm 0}$ = 50, 100, and 150 kyr.
P003_S0003.perturbed: %Particles were released from one scale height above the CPD midplane at initial times $t_{\rm 0}$ = 50, 100, and 150 kyr. We initially simulated 10 different release epochs but only include the three ($t_0$ = 50, 100, 150 kyr) that showed statistically significant trends in COM stability, omitting the others to maintain a clear narrative.

P004_S0004.quote: the conversion of ice to COMs is 100\% efficient for all particle sizes
P004_S0004.reason: The surrounding_context states thermal processing 'is the dominant pathway' and uses conditional language, while the original caption describes dotted lines highlighting portions that 'intersect' the COM zone, not claiming 100% efficiency.

P005_S0005.quote: the decoupling of large particles is the primary cause of the CPD's depletion over time
P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the causal direction: 'Since these release epochs occur later, the gas density in the CPD has decreased...As the gas density decreases, the Stokes number increases', not the reverse.
P005_S0005.original: The parameters adopted in Figs. \ref{fig:100kyr} and \ref{fig:150kyr} are identical to those used in Fig. \ref{fig:50kyr}, except that particles are released at $t_0 = 100$ kyr and $t_0 = 150$ kyr, respectively, during the evolution of the CPD. Since these release epochs occur later, the gas density in the CPD has decreased compared to the conditions at $t_0 = 50$ kyr.  As the gas density decreases, the Stokes number of the particles increases, since it is inversely proportional to the local gas density ($\mathrm{St} \propto 1/\rho_{\mathrm{gas}}$). This increase in the Stokes number results in weaker coupling between the particles and the gas. Consequently, at $t_0 = 100\,\mathrm{kyr}$, particles with sizes exceeding $100\,\mu\mathrm{m}$ are predominantly subject to gas drag, whereas at $t_0 = 150\,\mathrm{kyr}$, even particles as small as $1\,\mu\mathrm{m}$ exhibit inward drift, experiencing gas drag effects beyond $R_c$. At $t_0$ = 100 $\mathrm{kyr}$ and 150 $\mathrm{kyr}$, the thermal processing region shifts closer to Jupiter within the CPD, lying within approximately $\sim$$20\,R_{\mathrm{Jup}}$ during these stages of the disk's evolution. Ices composed of NH$_3$:CO$_2$, whether carried by grains influenced by gas drag or by the smallest particles tightly coupled to the gas and released below $R_c$, transit through this region. During this passage, thermal processing may convert these ices into COMs.
P005_S0005.perturbed: The parameters adopted in Figs. \ref{fig:100kyr} and \ref{fig:150kyr} are identical to those used in Fig. \ref{fig:50kyr}. As the Stokes number of the particles increases, we observe a corresponding decrease in the local gas density, suggesting that the decoupling of large particles is the primary cause of the CPD's depletion over time. Consequently, at $t_0 = 100\,\mathrm{kyr}$, the increased gas drag on $100\,\mu\mathrm{m}$ particles causes the gas density to drop further. At $t_0$ = 100 $\mathrm{kyr}$ and 150 $\mathrm{kyr}$, the shift of the thermal processing region closer to Jupiter is driven by the conversion of NH$_3$:CO$_2$ ices into COMs, which alters the disk's thermal structure.

P006_S0006.quote: proving that UV photolysis occurs before any thermal sublimation can take place
P006_S0006.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'most of these particles lose their CH3OH ice due to sublimation before reaching this threshold', directly contradicting the perturbed claim that UV photolysis precedes thermal sublimation.
P006_S0006.original: Interestingly, Fig.~\ref{fig:100kyr} indicates that particles released at $t_0 = 100\,\mathrm{kyr}$ beyond approximately 75 and 125 $R_{\mathrm{Jup}}$, with sizes of 1 $\mu\mathrm{m}$ and 100 $\mu\mathrm{m}$, respectively, follow trajectories that exceed the irradiation threshold defined by \citet{tenelanda2022}. However, as the figure shows, most of these particles lose their CH$_3$OH ice due to sublimation before reaching this threshold. A similar trend is observed in Fig.~\ref{fig:150kyr}, where the computed trajectories of 1--$\mu\mathrm{m}$ particles released at $t_0 = 150\,\mathrm{kyr}$ beyond approximately 85 $R_{\mathrm{Jup}}$ in the CPD reach the irradiation threshold, while most of them have lost their CH$_3$OH ice by that time.
P006_S0006.perturbed: Interestingly, Fig.~\ref{fig:100kyr} indicates that particles released at $t_0 = 100\,\mathrm{kyr}$ beyond approximately 75 and 125 $R_{\mathrm{Jup}}$ follow trajectories that exceed the irradiation threshold. We interpret this as evidence that irradiation is the primary mechanism for CH$_3$OH removal. A similar trend is observed in Fig.~\ref{fig:150kyr}, where the trajectories reach the irradiation threshold, proving that UV photolysis occurs before any thermal sublimation can take place.

P007_S0007.quote: we have adjusted the radial bins to only include those that showed a p-value < 0.05 for inward migration
P007_S0007.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages establish that all 14 uniformly distributed release points are used without selection, making this post-hoc p-value filtering a methodological flaw contradicting the stated approach.

P008_S0008.quote: the rapid migration of 1~cm particles causes the CPD evolution timestep to shorten to 1~yr
P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding_context (figure caption for distrib_v2) states '1~cm particles migrate on timescales shorter than the adopted CPD evolution timestep (1~yr)', meaning the timestep is fixed at 1 yr, not shortened by particle migration.

P009_S0009.quote: they are more likely to form COMs than the 1~$\mu$m particles, as their residence time in the zone is effectively infinite
P009_S0009.reason: The surrounding_context states '1~cm particles migrate on timescales shorter than the adopted CPD evolution timestep (1~yr)', indicating very short residence times, contradicting the perturbed claim of effectively infinite residence time.

P000_S0010.quote: with the smallest particles being removed fastest...1~cm particles exhibit much longer residence times
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'with the largest particles being removed fastest' and '1~cm particles exhibit much shorter residence times', directly contradicting both changed claims.
P000_S0010.original: Figure~\ref{fig:distrib} shows the distribution of residence times for particles within the COM thermal stability region, considering particles of sizes 1~$\mu$m and 1~cm, released into the CPD at $t_0$ = 50, 100, and 150~kyr after its formation. Both particle sizes have short residence times, with the largest particles being removed fastest. For 1~$\mu$m particles, the residence time distribution peaks at around 100~yr at $t_0$ = 50~kyr and shifts to approximately 200~yr at $t_0$ = 100 and 150~kyr. In contrast, 1~cm particles exhibit much shorter residence times. Those released at $t_0$ = 50~kyr typically remain within the thermal stability region of COMs for less than 30~yr, decreasing to under 4~yr at $t_0$ = 100~kyr. None of the 1~cm particles released at $t_0$ = 150~kyr remain in the stability region for more than 1~yr, which corresponds to the temporal resolution of the iterative scheme of our model.
P000_S0010.perturbed: Figure~\ref{fig:distrib} shows the distribution of residence times for particles within the COM thermal stability region, considering particles of sizes 1~$\mu$m and 1~cm, released into the CPD at $t_0$ = 50, 100, and 150~kyr after its formation. Both particle sizes have short residence times, with the smallest particles being removed fastest. For 1~$\mu$m particles, the residence time distribution peaks at around 100~yr at $t_0$ = 50~kyr and shifts to approximately 200~yr at $t_0$ = 100 and 150~kyr. In contrast, 1~cm particles exhibit much longer residence times. Those released at $t_0$ = 50~kyr typically remain within the thermal stability region of COMs for less than 30~yr, decreasing to under 4~yr at $t_0$ = 100~kyr. None of the 1~cm particles released at $t_0$ = 150~kyr remain in the stability region for more than 1~yr, which corresponds to the temporal resolution of the iterative scheme of our model.

\section{Discussion} 
\label{sec:sec4}

Overall, our simulations suggest that particles of varying sizes, released at different points during the evolution of the CPD, pass through regions where temperatures are favorable for COM synthesis. We focus our analysis exclusively on the $\alpha = 10^{-3}$ and $R_c = 50$ cases where significant residence times were observed, as these provide the most robust evidence for our hypothesis, while omitting the non-significant results from the high-turbulence $\alpha = 10^{-2}$ scenarios to clarify the presentation.
